summary: Gruber dissects a report from The Information revealing Apple is developing a custom AI server chip (codenamed Baltra) with Broadcom, expected for mass production by 2026. He argues the reporting's framing obscures that Apple is driving the Broadcom arrangement, not the reverse. More significantly, he highlights a buried detail: Apple cancelled development of a quad-Max 'Extreme' chip for Macs, reassigning its Israeli silicon team to AI chip work. Gruber connects this to a 2023 anonymous Apple engineer tip suggesting the quad chip was already cancelled through the M7 generation. He speculates this means a top-tier Mac Pro chip is years away, if it ever materializes, as Apple prioritizes AI infrastructure over high-end desktop silicon.
key_points: Apple is building a custom AI server chip (Baltra) with Broadcom networking technology, targeting 2026 mass production on TSMC's N3P process
Apple cancelled a quad-Max 'Extreme' M-series chip this past summer, redirecting Israeli silicon engineers to AI chip development
Gruber identifies Broadcom as the likely leak source based on the story's obsequious framing of Broadcom's role
The cancelled quad chip was likely M6 generation (2026-2027 products), not M4, since M4 designs were finalized long before summer 2024
A 2023 anonymous Apple GPU engineer corroborates that the quad chip was cancelled with no plans through at least M7, with multi-die packaging as a possible future alternative starting around M8
money_quotes: I'll go out on a limb and say that it's Apple choosing to take a different tack with Broadcom than Google did, rather than a choice in any way driven by Broadcom.
The hint of obsequiousness to Broadcom suggests to me, pretty clearly, that it's sources from Broadcom who provided the leaks for this story.

What really caught my eye in this report wasn't the AI server chips, but rather the following, included seemingly only as an aside even though I thought it was the most interesting nugget in the report.
Such a chip, available exclusively in the Mac Pro, would give the Mac Pro a much more obvious reason to exist alongside the Mac Studio.
I've had my fingers crossed that we'll also see an 'M4 Extreme' — or whatever Apple would decide to call a tier above 'Ultra' — sooner rather than later.
Additional details of the business arrangement couldn't be learned is some passive voice bullshit.
Today's report has me thinking, sadly, that could be a few years off, at the soonest.
key_insight: Apple's cancellation of its quad-Max chip to redirect engineers toward AI server silicon reveals a decisive strategic pivot prioritizing cloud AI infrastructure over high-end desktop performance.
